title: Estimating Stockout Costs and Optimal Stockout Rates: A Case on the Management of Ugly Produce Inventory
abstract: Efficiently managing inventories requires an accurate estimation of stockout costs. This estimation is complicated by challenges in determining how to compensate consumers monetarily to ensure they will maintain the same level of utility they would have obtained had stockouts not occurred. This paper presents an analysis of these compensation costs as applied to the design of optimal stockout rates by an online retailer marketing to consumers aesthetically substandard fruits and vegetables rejected by mainstream grocery chains. Because growers face high uncertainty in their harvesting conditions and in the aesthetic quality of their crops and there are little data on hand to predict the value consumers attach to the availability of subpar produce, it is difficult to optimally match the supply of these products with consumers‚Äô demand. Our analysis draws from a multiple discrete-continuous extreme value (MDCEV) choice model to calculate consumer compensations that the retailer can use to estimate the opportunity costs of stockouts to manage its inventory. We show that not taking into account these compensation costs could unduly inflate the optimal stockout rates for these products. Armed with these compensation cost estimates, we show how these costs can serve as incentives for retailers to source greater inventory amounts of imperfect produce from growers and how this will ultimately translate into less waste in the supply chain.

article.1. Introduction: Estimating stockout costs is critical to the effective management of retail inventories. Without knowing these costs, it is impossible to balance optimally the financial risks of holding inventories with the losses incurred from out-of-stocks. Relevant stockout costs includedirectlosses in profits caused by retailers‚Äô missed opportunities to sell out-of-stock items, as well as the losses incurredindirectlyfrom not being able to sell available stock-keeping units (SKUs) that are not purchased because other items are unavailable (Anderson et al. 2006). These losses can be readily estimated by subtracting retail prices and wholesale costs for the out-of-stock SKUs, as well as for the items that are in stock but not purchased when inventories for the out-of-stock items run out. Stockout costs also include long-run losses caused by erosion in consumer goodwill when products become unavailable. Offsetting these losses requires compensating consumers monetarily to ensure they maintain the same level of utility they would have obtained had the SKUs‚Äô stockouts not occurred. Thesecompensationcosts are much more challenging to quantify because they require modeling stockout effects on consumers‚Äô utility. Given this challenge, researchers have traditionally opted to model compensation costs using artificial penalties that reflect the loss of consumers‚Äô patronage because of stockouts (e.g.,Parlar 1988,Lippman and McCardle 1997,Netessine and Rudi 2003). These instruments are valuable because they capture richer relationships between inventory availability and profitability than those explained in simpler models. However, they do not capture the actual compensation costs sellers incur when they stock out (Dana and Petruzzi 2001). Compensation costs quantify the disutility consumers experience because of stockouts, which, in turn, can lead to an erosion in consumer goodwill, a decrease in consumer loyalty, and long-run losses in revenue for sellers (Borle et al. 2008). Our paper contributes a procedure for calculating these costs for individual products. Our procedure comprises three stages. In stage one, we specify a demand model that captures consumers‚Äô purchasing behavior involving baskets made of multiple SKUs in different quantities per SKU. In stage two, we numerically evaluate the indirect utility function by maximizing the utility attainable by each consumer from a full assortment given consumer budget constraints and limits on basket sizes. Then, in stage three, we calculate the compensation amounts needed to lift the reduced utility to the level obtained from the full assortment following the removal of an out-of-stock product from the assortment. With these compensation amounts, along with the other stockout cost components obtained from the SKU margins, we can estimate the SKUs‚Äô optimal stockout rates and determine the impact that each of these components has on the inventory amounts needed for the SKUs. To perform our analysis, we focused on the online retail sales of cosmetically inferior produce marketed to consumers in the United States via the internet. Over 40% of the food available for consumption in the United States goes uneaten every year (USDA Economic Research Service 2010), and about 20% of this waste involves fruits and vegetables that are left to rot upstream in the supply chain because they have not met the standards of grocery stores regarding color, size, shape, and time to ripeness (McPhillips 2018,Atkin 2019). In recent years, retailers such as Hungry Harvest and Misfits Market have sought to profit by addressing this problem. These firms have leveraged the internet to market directly to consumers a variety of ‚Äúugly‚ÄĚ fruits and vegetables that grocery chains have deemed inadequate for sale in their stores. Through their websites, these companies sell to consumers all forms of subpar produce sourced directly from local farmers with whom they have preestablished relationships. This ensures that, despite these products‚Äô cosmetic flaws, their freshness, flavor, and health benefits match or exceed those of standard products found traditionally in supermarkets. There are several idiosyncrasies in these retailers‚Äô business models that are particularly relevant to our analysis. First, the retailers sell their products in bundles that shoppers can customize in their orders based on the assortments available at the time of purchase. Thus, because an out-of-stock situation involving an item can affect the purchase intentions of other SKUs, we will be able to estimate the losses caused by missed opportunities to sell out-of-stock items, as well as the losses incurred from not selling SKUs that are in stock but not purchased when other items become unavailable. Second, the retailers change their assortments frequently to ensure inventory remains fresh and to encourage consumers to repurchase. The retailers‚Äô sourcing decisions are typically opportunistic, as they are subject to the availability of perishable produce rejected by grocery chains at discrete points in time, which increases the risks of stockouts. In addition, it is often difficult for retailers to source sufficient inventory from suppliers, as quantities available for these products are highly uncertain and retailers have a limited ability to incentivize growers to rescue larger amounts of rejected produce. Third, because these goods have not been traditionally marketed to consumers, there are little data available to predict the compensation amounts consumers may receive when stocks run out. This makes it difficult for retailers to estimate the long-run cost of lost business caused by the restrictions imposed on the availability of stocks because of limitations in supply accessibility. Intuitively, one could argue that these compensation costs may approach zero given these products‚Äô aesthetic inferiority relative to standard counterparts available at grocery chains. Evidence to the contrary, however, would underscore the need to account for these costs to estimate optimal stockout rates not only for the subpar produce sold by these retailers but also for these products‚Äô counterparts sold at grocery chains, as well as for other inferior goods, such as secondhand or refurbished products, sold by other firms. Compensation costs are important for retailers selling imperfect produce because losses in consumer goodwill caused by the disutility created by stockouts will easily lead to an erosion in consumer loyalty. For one, these retailers compete in an online environment, where transaction costs for consumers are low (Bakos 1997). As a result, consumers exposed to stockouts at a retailer could easily discontinue buying from this firm and switch to a competitor with superior inventory availability (Ansari et al. 2008). Moreover, consumers‚Äô participation in subpar produce markets is often experimental. Many consumers want to test whether the quality of the products they buy disconfirms negative preconceived perceptions regarding ugly produce (Loebnitz et al. 2015,Grewal et al. 2019). If the service that retailers offer when selling these products is deficient (because of stockouts), it will easily turn consumers away, back to buying from sellers offering grade A produce through traditional channels. This erosion in consumer loyalty will, in turn, undermine consumers‚Äô lifetime value, a critical contributor to long-term revenue for these retailers. Consumption of fruits and vegetables at these retailers (and elsewhere) typically follows a regular periodicity, as consumers grow accustomed to buying these products from the same sellers (Ehrenberg 1988). Retailers are counting on consumers to continue coming back to buy from them regularly as long as they are able to forestall erosion in consumer loyalty and prevent consumer departures because of stockouts. However, for that to occur, retailers would need to incur compensation costs. The need for compensation costs to address disutilities caused by stockouts is further amplified by the fact that consumers are buying products in bundles, making it necessary for retailers to manage access to inventory across multiple SKUs simultaneously. Estimating compensation costs will also help identify which SKUs demand lower optimal stockout rates and thus are subject to trade-offs favoring higher inventory levels over stockouts. Not taking into account these compensation costs could unduly inflate the optimal stockout rates for these SKUs. We find this inflation ranges from 7.22% to 130.83% (with an average of 43.97%) across SKUs. Our results also show that compensation costs often exceed direct and indirect costs. These findings underscore the importance of considering compensation costs in the estimation of optimal stockout rates. Because retailers selling subpar produce have traditionally been unable to incorporate compensation costs in their inventory decisions, their stockout rates often exceed optimal values. Armed with our compensation cost estimates, we show that retailers have an incentive to source greater inventory amounts from growers. In turn, if growers are able to obtain higher revenue from selling more subpar produce, they will have a greater incentive to rescue these items and invest in the activities necessary to bring these products to market. This will result in less waste upstream in the supply chain, along with a more effective use of resources in the farming of these products and lower greenhouse gases generated from the rotting of food in the field. Besides providing relevant insights to practice, our paper offers important contributions to methodology and theory. Methodologically, our paper contributes, to the estimation of stockout costs, the design of a procedure to calculate compensation costs based on a demand model that captures simultaneously the multiple discreteness and quantity decisions consumers make when purchasing SKU baskets. Current advances in choice modeling generally focus on discrete choices in high-dimensional (Jiang et al. 2024) or limited-data (Farias et al. 2013) settings. In so doing, we show how these measurements can be used in the design of inventory management policies and interventions to reduce supply chain waste, which may extend beyond our context to include other settings where products are sold in bundles. Our paper also contributes to inventory management theory. Although there is extensive literature dedicated to the development of optimal inventory policies, little work has investigated how customers respond to stockouts. Often, stockout costs are not fully known. The lack of comprehensive estimates of these costs is one of the reasons why many retailers have not been overly concerned with the implementation of optimal inventory models in practice (Raman 1999,Zipkin 2000,Anderson et al. 2006). By developing a procedure that can estimate stockout costs thoroughly for individual SKUs, our study seeks to address this deficiency. The emphasis on a methodology to analyze individual SKUs (rather than broad product categories, for example) is important because it can provide the flexibility for further analyses at more aggregated levels.
article.2. Related Literature: Our work is related to various streams of literature. The first comprises studies on the costs of stockouts and the effectiveness of various strategies for reducing these costs. Earlier studies analyzed the effects of stockouts on products‚Äô market shares (Charlton and Ehrenberg 1976) and consumers‚Äô store-switching behavior (Fitzsimons 2000). More recent studies have quantified the effects of stockouts on lost sales and consumers‚Äô lifetime values by estimating temporary price promotions to offset these effects (e.g.,Anderson et al. 2006,Musalem et al. 2010,Jing and Lewis 2011). However, unlike our study, they provide no guidance on how to identify the appropriate level of consumer compensation to restore utility when specific SKUs are out of stock, nor do they offer insights applicable to inferior products. Other studies have focused on the costs of assembling optimal product assortments in order to minimize stockouts (K√∂k and Fisher 2007,Rooderkerk et al. 2013). In these studies, stockout costs depend on how expensive it is to assemble these assortments. However, the supply of these assortments is typically assumed to be deterministic, which is not the case in our context. This is why, rather than focusing on assembling optimal assortments, we concentrate on estimating the stockout compensations consumers should receive for their utility to remain unchanged at the counterfactual level of full assortment. The second stream includes work that has used demand models to capture simultaneously multiple discreteness and quantity decisions made by consumers buying different SKUs in the same order. We use this model to estimate stockout compensation costs. Other authors, particularly in marketing, have used this model to measure customer lifetime values (Sunder et al. 2016), examine consumer spending allocations across apparel categories (Chen and Ngwe 2018), and analyze consumer choices among yogurt varieties and how these choices inform shelf space allocations (Kim et al. 2002). To our knowledge, we are the first to deploy this type of model in operations management to measure stockout costs and inform inventory policies. The third stream includes studies that examine the management of inventory for inferior goods. Our analysis of consumer compensation costs during out-of-stock events involving imperfect produce and their impact on inventory performance extends research that has identified drivers of consumer valuation of inferior products and its impact across the supply chain (Vorasayan and Ryan 2006,Ovchinnikov 2011,Subramanian and Subramanyam 2012). This research has also considered the role of inferior goods‚Äô inventories in consumers‚Äô valuations of these products‚Äô standard counterparts as well as in reducing stockout occurrences when inventories for these counterparts run out (Zhang and Zhang 2018,Singh et al. 2022). Despite the volume of work in this literature, we are unaware of studies that have estimated optimal stockout rates based on the costs necessary to compensate consumers for stockouts involving inferior products. Our focus on estimating these costs in the context of below-grade produce also contributes to the literature that has examined subjective consumer valuations of subpar groceries. The work ofCardello (1994)andHurling and Shepherd (2003)shows that sensory attributes contribute to the rejection or acceptance of food items by consumers and that the visual appearance of these goods influences consumer preferences. Building on these insights,Loebnitz et al. (2015)andGrewal et al. (2019)show that consumers have a low valuation for subpar groceries. We show that despite this low valuation, the costs incurred by retailers to compensate consumers when below-grade fruits and vegetables stock out are significant and often exceed the margins retailers lose from these out-of-stocks. These insights contribute to research in operations management that has studied inventory performance at the grocery retail level. This work has focused on evaluating the effects on inventory performance of different types of contracts between grocers and suppliers (Dong et al. 2014,Lim et al. 2022), in-store logistics (Reiner et al. 2013), product expiration (Akkas et al. 2019), and shelf-space and product display allocations (Pak et al. 2020). Our findings extend this research by assessing the extent to which compensation costs justify lower stockout rates in grocery retail settings.
article.3. Background and Data: We conducted our study in partnership with Imperfect Foods (IF), an online retailer selling below-grade produce to consumers across independently managed markets in the United States. In each of these markets, IF sources stocks from growers and assembles assortments for sale weekly. These assortments cannot be set in advance because growers have no systematic control over the aesthetic quality of their products and cannot guarantee that they can match demands for specific product varieties marketed by IF. Thus, IF‚Äôs weekly assortment decisions are typically opportunistic. 3.1. Consumer Shopping Journey To buy at IF, every consumer must first create an account and provide their home address, payment method, and other personal information. A consumer must order from IF on a preset weekly schedule (Figure 1(a)). However, consumer orders need not adhere to a specific frequency. They can be placed every week, every two weeks, et cetera. Furthermore, orders do not have to follow a fixed cadence. Consumers can skip orders for specific weeks (Figure 1(a)), decrease their order frequency, or suspend future orders altogether without incurring any penalties (Figure 1(b)). Every time consumers place an order, they must log in and make their selections from the assortment offered by IF to their specific market. Based on this assortment, the consumers can select the SKUs and the number of units per SKU they wish to purchase by adding them to a virtual shopping box until the box is full. As shown inFigure 1(b), consumers have the option of assembling different box sizes and including in their boxes different combinations of organic and conventional fruits and vegetables. Note that IF may add labels (e.g., HOT DEAL) to some SKUs to increase their visibility and influence consumer choices. IF also ‚Äúpreloads‚ÄĚ SKUs in consumers‚Äô boxes; however, consumers can easily replace these SKUs with other products to customize their orders. As they assemble the boxes, consumers see the price of every SKU in the assortment and the apportionment used by IF to sell the SKU. Not all SKUs are sold using the same type of apportionment. IF sells some SKUs by weight (e.g., pounds) and others by count (e.g., bunches). Please seeFigure 2(a)for examples. Moreover, depending on each SKU‚Äôs apportionment, IF assigns a ‚Äúunit value‚ÄĚ to the SKU that the consumer can use to determine how much capacity the SKU would take up once it is in the box. For instance, as shown inFigure 2(b), IF sells organic carrots by the pound and assigns a value of one unit to each pound of this SKU a consumer adds to the box. The sum of the unit values of all the SKUs consumers add to their box (i.e., the number of ‚Äúboxed units‚ÄĚ) cannot be greater than the total capacity of the box. As consumers assemble their boxes, IF also provides them with the total amount of money to pay for the box. As is well-known, consumers typically set a mental budget when purchasing groceries (Heath and Soll 1996,Heilman et al. 2002). Therefore, they use this information to guide their shopping and are likely to resist buying more items once their budgets are met. Ultimately, these budget constraints and the size of the boxes will guide consumers‚Äô decisions on which products to buy and in what amounts. Finally, once all the SKUs are in the box, consumers must pay for the products minus any discounts. IF offered discounts through coupons applicable to entire orders, not specific products. Moreover, for every order, consumers must pay a fixed shipping and handling fee ($4.99). InFigure 2(b), for example, the screenshot shows that the total value of the basket is $22.53 with no discount and a delivery charge of $4.99. Finally, as shown inFigure 2(c), all boxes are shipped to consumers on the same day of the week (usually on Tuesdays or Thursdays). Consumers can place their orders up to a day before the boxes are scheduled to ship out from the IF facility serving their market. 3.2. Data Description Our data focus on the San Francisco Bay Area (SFBA) market of IF, as this was the market where IF started operations (in December 2015) and had served the largest number of consumers at the time we conducted our study. The data span a 23-week period between October 1, 2016, and February 28, 2017. AsFigure 3shows, SKU counts in the assortment had stabilized in this market at around an average of 38 per week at the start of our analysis period (week 39 inFigure 3) and remained so until the end of the period (week 61). From our data collection, we can address three identification issues regarding possible assortment and price endogeneity. First, despite the weekly SKU counts stabilizing around an average of 38, the number and composition of SKUs in the assortment continued changing during the analysis.Figure 3shows significant fluctuations in the number of SKUs that IF ordered per week, whereasFigure 4shows decreasing overlaps in the composition of assortments between consecutive weeks, suggesting weekly assortments at IF are exogenous. These conditions exist because goods are highly perishable and IF must replace them opportunistically on a regular basis with surpluses available from farmers. Second, IF‚Äôs managers confirmed that, at the time of our study, the company set SKU prices independently of the inventory amounts sourced from farmers. Price was based on a markup amount on the wholesale price IF paid for each SKU, and this amount was independent of the amount of inventory IF purchased for each SKU. In addition, IF did not engage in sales-dependent strategic price setting given the company‚Äôs short trajectory in the market at the time of our study. Thus, the markups chosen by IF were generally independent of the prices of SKUs at retail stores and the demand in the SFBA market. These conditions allow us to circumvent concerns regarding assortment and price endogeneity in our study. Third, the wholesale prices IF paid for the SKUs are largely independent of consumer demand shocks. This is mainly because IF is typically sourced from small local farmers with little leverage to manipulate prices based on consumer demand fluctuations, particularly for subpar crops for which they have limited market options. Similar conditions were observed byShimomura and Thisse (2012)andParenti (2018)in market competition studies involving large versus small firms. IF provided us with transaction information, including consumer identification (ID), order ID, date of purchase, order discount amount, and weekly assortment. IF also provided basket-level information, including product names, ordered quantities (number of boxed units), prices, and apportionments. The data include 207 SKUs and 18,314 transactions by 2,153 consumers. Each of these consumers placed at least five orders at IF during our analysis. We specify this sampling criterion to ensure that every consumer has enough repeated observations to generate reliable parameter estimates. Our sample of consumers accounts for almost 80% of IF‚Äôs revenue in the SFBA market. In all the orders they placed, consumers bought exclusively small-sized boxes of organic produce. Table 1provides a distribution of the consumers in our sample based on their frequency of purchase at IF (weekly, biweekly, monthly, and irregularly). In all, about 21% of consumers did not purchase regularly at IF. For these consumers, the longest interpurchase interval was three weeks. Based on these observations and given that IF manages its operations (e.g., procuring of inventory, delivering orders to customers) on a weekly basis, we conduct our analysis at the weekly level.Table 2provides summary statistics of the sampled consumers‚Äô shopping activity at IF. We observe that, on average, consumers bought 10 boxed units and spent $13.67 per order (excluding the shipping and handling fee). They also received an average order discount of $0.20, which represents a small portion of their average order expenditure ($0.20/$13.67‚ÄČ=‚ÄČ1.46%). We also see that, on average, consumers purchased about nine times during our focal period of 23‚ÄČweeks, approximating a biweekly purchase pattern. Furthermore, the average price per SKU is $1.38 (per boxed unit) and is relatively stable over time, as reflected by the low standard deviation of 0.20. This is consistent with our earlier observations that IF does not engage in strategic pricing. We expand on these statistics inFigure 5. This figure shows the distributions in order size and order scope across all consumer purchases in our sample. According to these distributions, more than 97.5% of orders include at least six boxed units whereas more than 48% include at least six SKUs. This evidence, combined with the statistics inTable 2showing that the average order size (10.02 boxed units) is higher than the average order scope (6.28 SKUs), suggests that consumers typically purchase multiple SKUs in different quantities per order. The distributions inFigure 5also show that there is sufficient variation in order size and order scope to estimate the discrete SKU choices and quantities in the demand model. Moreover, as shown inFigure 5, the spread of the order size distribution is wider than that of the order scope, providing more information (in terms of variation) to estimate the quantity component of our model. However, despite this variation, reduced form evidence (in Online Appendix A) shows a positive link between weekly assortment size and the number of consumers buying per week. Thus, the scope of SKUs in consumers‚Äô orders appears to be a relevant attribute in the choices consumers make when buying at IF.
article.4. Choice Model: Section 3and Online Appendix A‚Äôs evidence of a link between assortment size and consumers‚Äô choices to patronize IF, as well as evidence that consumers choose to buy at IF bundles made of different SKUs and quantities per SKU underscore the need to analyze these decisions based on a model that accounts simultaneously for the multiple SKU discreteness and quantities in these choices. To build this model, we cannot draw on discrete choice models and their variants because these are constrained in two aspects. First, although they allow for the capturing of consumers‚Äô choice of multiple SKUs in a set, they do not allow for the modeling of quantity decisions for each SKU selected. Furthermore, they do not account for satiation effects, prevalent in our setting. Consider what happens when a consumer buys a box of produce. In this box, the consumer can choose the variety of items (e.g., orange, cucumber, apple) and the quantity of each item (e.g., two oranges, five cucumbers, four apples). However, at the same time, the marginal utility the consumer obtains from the SKUs will decrease as greater quantities of each SKU are chosen. Based on these conditions, consumer choice at a retailer such as IF is characterized by multiple discreteness situations where individuals select, from an array of SKUs, one or more alternatives in different quantities depending on theirbaseline utilityand level ofsatiationfor each SKU, all the while adhering to their own budget constraints and the volume limitations imposed by IF‚Äôs preset box size. To model such situations, we use the multiple discrete-continuous extreme value (MDCEV) econometric model developed byBhat (2005,2008). Based on this model, we account for the multiple discrete-continuous nature of consumer demand. In addition, we allow for the presence of multiple constraints (i.e., budget and volume constraints) by employing the approach ofSatomura et al. (2011).Section 4.1develops our choice model based on a constrained utility maximization framework (Kim et al. 2002), specifying consumer utility and decision constraints. Then, we apply the Kuhn-Tucker conditions inSection 4.2to derive the model likelihood function for estimating consumer utility. 4.1. Constrained Utility Maximization We assume that an observed box order reflects a customer‚Äôs optimal choice of multiple products along with their quantities. Given a limited budget (S) and capacity in the box (C), the customer‚Äôs goal is to maximize the overall utility while satisfying both the budget and capacity constraints: Consistent withBhat (2008), we specify the functional form of consumer utility based on a generalized variant of the translated constant elasticity of substitution (CES) utility function: For outside goods, our model follows closelySatomura et al. (2011)‚Äôs approach for multiple constraints. Because the outside goods are assumed to be always consumed (i.e.,xzt>‚ÄČ0 andxwt>‚ÄČ0), there is no need for a translation parameter (ő≥). Consequently, the utility functions for the outside goods are simplified as follows: Following the preliminary evidence from Online Appendix A, our utility model evaluates the impact of weekly assortment size on consumers‚Äô utility for the outside good. In so doing, the model allows for zero spending of the inside goods through the outside good such that if a consumer chooses to purchase no inside goods in a given week, that customer will choose to buy the outside good. Note that it is common practice in a discrete choice framework to incorporate common factors influencing the latent utilities of all inside goods into the utility of the outside good. This modeling approach can be justified because only differences in utility matter in latent utility models like the MDCEV model (Train 2009, p. 19). Alternatively, if consumers do choose to buy inside goods in a given week, they may spend part of their budget on these goods and allocate the rest to the outside good. In addition to weekly assortment size, the model covariates include the discount amount applied to a consumer order and the recency of consumer purchases (measured based on a lagged purchase indicator). These covariates have been shown to influence consumers‚Äô utility for the outside good (K√∂k et al. 2015,Chen and Ngwe 2018). By assuming independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) extreme value errors, we derive the closed-form model likelihood as follows: Furthermore, we allow each consumerhto have unique parameter values by defining an overarching random-effects distribution to capture the heterogeneity of individual-level parameters across the population: We employ a Bayesian inference approach for the model estimation. FollowingRossi et al. (2012), we use a hierarchical Bayes model to capture consumer heterogeneity and obtain consumer-level estimates of the utility parameters. We use a hierarchical approach because it enables us to leverage the information we have at the individual transaction level (average of 9.37 transactions per consumer) across a large number of individuals (2,153 consumers) to estimate heterogeneous effects (i.e., individual-specific coefficients) based on a specified (e.g., normal) distribution (Rossi and Allenby 2003). This Bayesian approach provides an exact and simple way to obtain individual-level parameters and stands in contrast with the classical Frequentist approach, which must rely on a simulation or an approximation method to obtain individual-level parameters (Allenby and Rossi 1998). For this reason, our approach has been more commonly used for the estimation of multiple discrete/continuous models, particularly in marketing (see, for example,Kim et al. 2002,2007;Satomura et al. 2011;Lee et al. 2013).2 We estimate our utility parameters using a likelihood function inEquation (8), a heterogeneity distribution inEquation (10), and a diffuse (or noninformative) prior distribution for the hyperparameters (Rossi et al. 2012). By using a noninformative prior distribution, we minimize the influence of the prior distribution on our estimates. Using a diffuse prior is standard practice in Bayesian estimation and used in prior applications of discrete/continuous choice models (e.g.,Allenby and Rossi 1998;Kim et al. 2002,2007;Satomura et al. 2011;Lee et al. 2013).3 We estimate the model using a Bayesian Markov chain Monte Carlo method that utilizes both Gibbs sampling and Metropolis-Hastings algorithms to evaluate the posterior distribution of our model parameters (Metropolis et al. 1953). For our analysis, we discarded the first 150,000 draws of the chain as a burn-in period and used the next 150,000 iterations to produce the parameter estimates. We thinned the chain by keeping every 100th draw to reduce the autocorrelation of sequential draws (Geyer 1992,Raftery and Lewis 1992). Online Appendix E details this procedure. To reduce the computational burden, we estimate our model by focusing on the top 50 SKUs (ūĚĎė=1,‚Ä¶,50k=1,‚Ä¶,50) based on purchase volume at IF and a composite product that aggregates the 51st to the 207th SKU (k=‚ÄČ51). Note, however, that our approach is scalable to include a wider assortment. Although the utilities of some products might be interdependent (e.g., complements or substitutes), incorporating cross-category relationships in an MDCEV model significantly increases the complexity of the model and its estimation, especially in a high-dimensional problem like ours (Bhat et al. 2015). Furthermore, as this complexity increases, the model‚Äôs estimation becomes intractable. Suppose, for example, that we allow for the interaction among all 50 inside goods in our analysis. This will result in 1,225 additional coefficients (C(n,r): 50 combination 2) to be estimated.4According toBhat (2008), there is no closed-form solution when this many interaction terms are allowed in the model. Online Appendix F expands on the implications for our analysis from assuming an independent utility structure relative to interdependence. The Online Appendix shows it is unlikely that assuming an independent utility structure for all 50 inside goods biases our results.

TheūĚõľ*őĪ*label on they-axis inFigure 6denotes the degree of the satiation effect, so smaller (or more negative) values imply a higher degree of satiation. On the other hand, theūĚúď*Ōą*label on thex-axis denotes the baseline utility so that larger values imply higher utility. Baseline utility (ūĚúď*Ōą*) affects the likelihood of an item being purchased (or purchase incidence). The higher the baseline utility, the greater the likelihood of the item being purchased. In turn, satiation (ūĚõľ*őĪ*) affects the conditional purchase quantity of an SKU given a purchase occasion. The higher the satiation associated with an SKU, the smaller the SKU quantity purchased. The pattern illustrated inFigure 6represents the estimated outcome from our data. Based on the same figure, we observe that the bottom right quadrant comprises products that have high baseline utility and high satiation. Because these products have a high likelihood of being included in consumers‚Äô boxes and are purchased in small quantities, they contribute to expanding variety in the bundles purchased by consumers. On the other hand, the upper left quadrant comprises products that have low baseline utility and low satiation. Therefore, products in this quadrant have a lower likelihood of being included in consumers‚Äô boxes. However, when they are included, they are usually purchased in larger quantities and therefore contribute to increasing the number of units per SKU in the bundles purchased by consumers. The intuition behind these results is that because SKUs with higher baseline utility also have a higher purchase incidence, consumers will tend to buy them in smaller quantities given their constraints. Examples of products with high baseline utility and high satiation in our sample include organic bacon avocados and organic apples. By contrast, because SKUs with lower baseline utility have a lower purchase incidence, consumers will tend to buy them in larger quantities during each purchase occasion. Examples of products with low baseline utility and low satiation in our sample include organic red bunched beets and organic cantaloupe. Our analysis also considered the potential effects caused by differences in SKU online displays on consumers‚Äô choices. Items could be given higher prominence by having a ‚ÄúHOT DEAL‚ÄĚ label displayed next to them. However, we observed that only three out of all 50 inside goods in our analysis were tagged with this label. Moreover, these items were tagged mostly during the first four weeks of our analysis period. Therefore, the use of this tag by IF was rare, representing only 2.74% of the selection instances. This lack of temporal and SKU variation in prominence makes it difficult to disentangle the two effects (i.e., utility and prominence effect) in the estimates. Nevertheless, our SKU and customer-level estimation, along with our inclusion of monthly time dummies, should, at least partially, account for the prominence effect during the first month when the ‚ÄúHOT DEAL‚ÄĚ labels were displayed. Based on this examination and the data we used for the analysis, the prominence of items has a minimal impact on our estimates. 5.3. Compensating Variation Analysis: Armed with the SKU-level parameter estimates for baseline utility and satiation effects, we use a compensating variation analysis to estimate the monetary amount that customers should receive in order to offset their loss of goodwill when an SKU is out of stock. This amount (known as the compensating value, or CV) will guarantee that customers will maintain the same level of utility they would have obtained had the stockout not occurred. We focus on estimating the CV for each SKU in the top 50 group, as well as the composite representing all other SKUs. The procedure for calculating the CVs comprises two stages. In the first stage, we numerically evaluate the indirect utility function by maximizing the utility attainable by each consumer from a full assortment given budget and quantity constraints. Then, in the second stage, we calculate the monetary amount required to lift the reduced utility following the removal of an SKU from the assortment to the utility level obtained from the full assortment. We express the indirect utility function for the first stage as We use theconstrOptimpackage for nonlinear optimization in R software to evaluate the indirect utility functions inEquations (11)and (12). The resultingūĚź∂‚ĀĘūĚĎČ(ūĚĎė)‚Ąé‚ĀĘūĚĎ°C‚ĀĘVh‚ĀĘt(k)for productkcan be interpreted as the amount by which consumerh‚Äôs budget would need to increase on purchase occasiontso that that consumer‚Äôs utility remains unchanged by the removal of productkfrom the retailer‚Äôs assortment. This increase is equivalent to a monetary compensation consumers would receive at the time of purchase, which they may or may not use to pay for their purchase at IF. For instance, this compensation may be offered through discounts applied to the consumers‚Äô current baskets or in equivalent cash value that consumers may or may not use to buy at IF. We then aggregate theūĚź∂‚ĀĘūĚĎČ(ūĚĎė)‚Ąé‚ĀĘūĚĎ°C‚ĀĘVh‚ĀĘt(k)over purchase occasionsūĚź∂‚ĀĘūĚĎČ(ūĚĎė)‚Ąé=‚ąĎūĚĎá‚ĄéūĚĎ°=1ūĚź∂‚ĀĘūĚĎČ(‚Ąé)‚Ąé‚ĀĘūĚĎ°C‚ĀĘVh(k)=‚ąĎt=1ThC‚ĀĘVh‚ĀĘt(h). However, we note that the CVs derived fromEquation (12)do not account for consumers‚Äô intrinsic affinity to IF, as reflected in their continuity in expenditures at IF. It is possible, for instance, that CVs for consumers with greater affinity are lower than those with lower affinity because the former are less sensitive to stockouts. To account for these differences, we express the CV of each product as a percentage of cumulative expenditures (PCV) at IF for each consumer.9Specifically, we divide each CV by the total spending of each consumer during their tenure at IF (seeEquation (13)). This way, high-affinity consumers, who are likely to have accumulated larger expenditures at IF, will generally produce lower PCVs (because of a larger denominator inEquation (13)) relative to those produced by low-affinity consumers. FollowingEquation (13), we average the PCV estimates across consumers to obtain the SKU-level PCVs listed in Online Appendix J and summarized graphically inFigure 8. Optimal Stockout Probability Model: Having estimated the average PCVs for the SKUs in our analysis, we can now proceed to model the optimal stockout probabilities of the SKUs while accounting for the appropriate level of consumer compensation when specific products are out of stock. An SKU‚Äôs optimal stockout probability depends on the extent to which the opportunity cost of stocking out the SKU exceeds the wholesale cost of the SKU. This is intuitive because, in this case, the savings from not increasing the SKU‚Äôs inventory are outweighed by the opportunity cost incurred when the SKU is out of stock. The classic newsvendor inventory management formulation for the optimal stockout probability (ő∂) provides a formal specification of this trade-off (see, for example,Porteus 2002, p. 16): Thecuterm also includes the retailer‚Äôs direct and indirect costs. The direct cost for an SKU corresponds to the difference between the SKU‚Äôs price and wholesale cost. As we explained in the introduction, this corresponds to the margin (ūĚĎÄūĚĎéūĚĎüūĚĎĒūĚĎĖūĚĎõ=ūĚĎÉūĚĎüūĚĎĖūĚĎźūĚĎí‚ąíūĚĎä‚ĄéūĚĎúūĚĎôūĚĎíūĚĎ†ūĚĎéūĚĎôūĚĎíūĚź∂ūĚĎúūĚĎ†ūĚĎ°Margin=Price‚ąíWholesaleCost) of the out-of-stock SKU. article.8. Discussion and Conclusion: In general, estimating the appropriate amount of compensation to give consumers when products stock out is a difficult problem not only because of a lack of quality data but also because of the inherent endogeneity that exists among price, assortments, and demand, which could bias utility estimates and the data structure necessary to obtain reliable stockout cost estimates. This problem is particularly acute for retailers selling inferior goods, given that there is often little data available to predict consumers‚Äô valuations of this type of products and to estimate the opportunity costs of lost business. Our analysis based on a retailer (Imperfect Foods) marketing inferior goods (ugly produce) contributes a utility model that can be applied to estimate stockout costs in other retail settings involving similar products (e.g., refurbished, secondhand items). Moreover, our focus on this retailer allows us to circumvent the abovementioned endogeneity issues and allow for more reliable stockout cost estimates, not only because changes in assortments at the retailer are exogenous but also because the retailer does not engage in sales-dependent strategic price setting to market its products and sets product prices independently of inventory amounts sourced from suppliers. In addition, we contribute to the literature an analytical approach incorporating an MDCEV choice model and a CV estimation procedure. This approach is particularly useful for retailers operating in similar contexts as ours, where consumers buy baskets with multiple products in different quantities. Because our results show that compensation costs caused by stockouts are nontrivial, Imperfect Foods and other retailers have a greater incentive to source more subpar produce to avoid stockouts. These retailers can use our stockout cost estimates and procedures to better inform their inventory management policies. Moreover, armed with our stockout cost estimates, the retailers now have a more accurate picture of which items are costlier to stock out. Higher stockout costs for these items mean lower optimal stockout probabilities. Therefore, retailers would need to maintain higher levels of inventory for these items. In turn, if growers are able to obtain higher revenue from selling more subpar produce, they will have a greater incentive to rescue these items and invest in the activities necessary to bring these products to market (e.g., picking, packing, and shipping to retailers). Our findings also have implications for inventory planning. First, we find that broader assortments significantly increase purchase incidence rates (seeTable 4). Second, our results (Figure 9) can help identify which SKUs are more costly to stock out and why. In virtually all cases, stockout costs are mainly caused by expenditures in consumers‚Äô compensations necessary to ensure consumers maintain the same utility levels they would have obtained had SKUs‚Äô stockouts not occurred. These compensation amounts significantly exceed the margins (price‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČwholesale cost) that would have been earned from selling the SKUs had stockouts not occurred. In only one case in our sample is the stockout cost for an SKU driven mainly by the margin loss from the out-of-stock SKU. These insights can inform retailers‚Äô optimal stockout probabilities and guide them in identifying which items they need to stock in greater versus lower amounts. On average, the optimal stockout probability (ő∂) across all inside goods analyzed equaled 0.25. Among these items, for instance, organic yellow squash (ūĚúĀ=0.13ő∂=0.13), organic green leaf lettuce (ūĚúĀ=0.13ő∂=0.13), and organic Yukon potatoes (ūĚúĀ=0.15ő∂=0.15) exhibit the lowest optimal stockout probabilities, whereas organic mixed mini heirloom tomatoes, organic red seedless grapes, and organic lacinato kale (ūĚúĀ=0.35,0.40,ő∂=0.35,0.40,and 0.63, respectively) exhibit the highest (Figure 10). Our results from the MDCEV analysis can also inform on the potential reductions in waste that are available to retailers from increasing their efforts to rescue greater amounts of subpar produce in order to reduce the compensation costs they give consumers when these products stock out. We know from the results summarized inFigure 11that ignoring stockout compensation costs estimated from the MDCEV analysis inflates optimal stockout probabilities by an average of about 44%. To eliminate this bias, there is an opportunity for retailers to increase by this same percentage the amount of subpar crops rescued upstream in the supply chain. According to our analysis inFigures 9and11, these amounts would exceed significantly the amounts rescued if retailers were to reduce only direct or indirect stockout costs. Our findings can also inform government interventions to broaden consumer access to ugly produce and reduce waste. Compensation-cost estimates could inform public policy programs directed at increasing the amounts of ugly produce rescued. Programs could use these estimates to design incentives intended to reward retailers like IF for absorbing the risk of increasing inventories to levels high enough to prevent consumer utility from eroding. Programs could also draw on the compensation-cost estimates to design tax incentives to encourage growers to collect below-grade produce for sale to IF or other retailers. The value of produce recovered upstream in the supply chain, and the opportunity to divert it to prevent stockouts that could undermine consumer welfare, justifies the loss in tax revenue that, but for the incentives, would be collected as government revenue (Evans and Nagele 2018). Moreover, public programs could use subsidies to fund the marketing and distribution of specific subpar crops. These programs may target particular products that require significant amounts of farming resources and are also responsible for large amounts of greenhouse gas emissions. Of particular interest among these products are items that exhibit high optimal stockout probabilities and for which retailers may be reluctant to source large inventory amounts. Interventions could also involve consumer education programs targeted at increasing public awareness of the benefits of buying imperfect fruits and vegetables, particularly those that have low optimal stockout probabilities and high environmental impact. The general public is often unaware that despite these products‚Äô aesthetic flaws, their freshness, flavor, and health benefits often match or exceed those of products found in mainstream grocery chains. These education programs may contribute to increasing the value consumers derive from buying imperfect produce by offering them information on how best to use these products to prepare meals at home. Programs such as these have long been known to be quite effective across broad consumer demographics (Bunn et al. 1990). Therefore, programs conducive to a greater appreciation for these products by consumers will improve retailers‚Äô conditions to market a higher volume of subpar produce. Our study forms the basis for several opportunities for future research in the operations management domain. First, although we show in Online Appendix F that the omission of complementarity and substitutability in the MDCEV model has limited impact on our results, future research can account for these effects more explicitly. However, note that because of the hierarchical structure of the MDCEV model, we do allow for preference correlation across individual consumers. Moreover, whereas we allow the parameters for baseline utility and satiation to be individual specific, our model does not consider consumers‚Äô price response, which is known to vary across individuals. Future research may incorporate price response as a parameter in the model. Additional opportunities also exist to further understand consumer heterogeneity. This could include analyses that use demographic data and other household/customer attributes (Bhat 2005) and/or location data to proxy socioeconomic conditions (Kim et al. 2002). Additional studies could also model more formally consumers‚Äô responses to substitution and complementarity effects to enhance the operationalization of indirect costs because of stockouts. In the paper, we used a data-driven approach for this operationalization. This approach builds on a market-basket analysis (Han et al. 2011,Aguinis et al. 2013) that has been used widely in quantitative marketing to define substitutes and complements in multiproduct and multicategory choice models (e.g.,Russell and Petersen 2000). One of the major advantages of this approach is that it does not rely on untestable model assumptions and is parsimonious (Aguinis et al. 2013). Finally, the estimation of the baseline utility function could be expanded to account for the effects that shipping fees and delivery lead times (Lim et al. 2024) may have on consumers‚Äô purchasing decisions. We did not include shipping fees and lead times as variables in our model, as we focus only on a single market area where there was limited variation in the fees and lead times to return reliable estimates. However, the impact of constant shipping cost (i.e., $4.99) and lead times (i.e., box shipping on the same day of the week) are captured in the outside option‚Äôs baseline utility. We hope that our study provides a springboard for further use of discrete/continuous choice models for various applications in operations management.
